title: Blinded
summary: A blinded creature can't see and automatically fails any ability check that requires sight.
content:
## Blinded

- A blinded creature can't see and automatically fails any ability check that requires sight.
- Attack rolls against the creature have **advantage**, and the creature's attack rolls have **disadvantage**.

### Common Sources
- *Blindness/Deafness* spell
- *Color Spray* spell
- Darkness (if the creature lacks darkvision or other special senses)
- Being in a heavily obscured area
- Some monster abilities (e.g., a Basilisk's Petrifying Gaze can blind before petrifying)

title: Charmed
summary: A charmed creature can't attack the charmer or target them with harmful abilities or magical effects.
content:
## Charmed

- A charmed creature can't attack the charmer or target the charmer with harmful abilities or magical effects.
- The charmer has **advantage** on any ability check to interact socially with the creature.

### Common Sources
- *Charm Person* spell
- *Hypnotic Pattern* spell
- Vampire's Charm ability
- Succubus/Incubus Charm
- Some Fey creatures

### Important Notes
- The charmed creature is NOT under the charmer's control — it simply can't harm the charmer and is more susceptible to persuasion.
- Many charm effects end if the charmer (or their allies) harm the target.

title: Deafened
summary: A deafened creature can't hear and automatically fails any ability check that requires hearing.
content:
## Deafened

- A deafened creature can't hear and automatically fails any ability check that requires hearing.

### Common Sources
- *Blindness/Deafness* spell (when deafness is chosen)
- Thunderwave and other thunder damage spells (some may deafen)
- Extremely loud environments

### Important Notes
- Deafened does NOT impose disadvantage on attacks (unlike Blinded).
- Spells with verbal components can still be cast while deafened.
- A deafened creature can't hear verbal commands or warnings.

title: Exhaustion
summary: Exhaustion has six cumulative levels, each adding penalties on top of previous ones. Level 6 means death.
content:
## Exhaustion (2014 Rules)

Some special abilities and environmental hazards lead to exhaustion. Exhaustion is measured in six levels.

| Level | Effect |
|---|---|
| 1 | Disadvantage on ability checks |
| 2 | Speed halved |
| 3 | Disadvantage on attack rolls and saving throws |
| 4 | Hit point maximum halved |
| 5 | Speed reduced to 0 |
| 6 | **Death** |

### Key Rules
- Effects are **cumulative**. A creature at level 2 has disadvantage on checks AND halved speed.
- If an already-exhausted creature suffers another effect that causes exhaustion, its current level increases by the amount specified.
- One level of exhaustion is removed after finishing a **long rest** with food and drink.
- The *Greater Restoration* spell reduces exhaustion by one level.

### Common Causes
- Going without food or water
- Forced marching beyond 8 hours
- Some spells (e.g., *Sickening Radiance*)
- Berserker Barbarian's Frenzy feature
- Extreme heat or cold (failed Constitution saves)

title: Exhaustion (2024 Rules)
summary: In 2024 rules, exhaustion uses 10 levels with a scaling penalty to d20 rolls instead of stacking discrete effects.
content:
## Exhaustion (2024 Rules)

Exhaustion in the 2024 revised rules works differently from the 2014 version.

### How It Works
- Exhaustion is measured on a scale of **1 to 10**.
- Each level of exhaustion imposes a **-2 penalty** to all d20 rolls (ability checks, attack rolls, and saving throws).
- At **level 10**, you **die**.

### Key Differences from 2014
- **Simpler**: One scaling penalty instead of six different effects.
- **More levels**: 10 levels instead of 6, making it more granular.
- **Less punishing early on**: Level 1 is just -2 to d20 rolls (vs disadvantage on all checks in 2014).
- **No speed reduction or HP max reduction** at intermediate levels.

### Recovery
- One level of exhaustion is removed after finishing a **long rest** with food and drink.
- The *Greater Restoration* spell removes exhaustion entirely.
- The *Lesser Restoration* spell reduces exhaustion by one level.

title: Frightened
summary: A frightened creature has disadvantage on ability checks and attack rolls while the source of its fear is within line of sight.
content:
## Frightened

- A frightened creature has **disadvantage** on ability checks and attack rolls while the source of its fear is within line of sight.
- The creature can't willingly move closer to the source of its fear.

### Common Sources
- *Cause Fear* spell
- *Fear* spell
- Dragon's Frightful Presence
- Wraith's Life Drain (on failed save)
- Paladin's Conquest subclass features

### Important Notes
- The creature CAN still take actions, including attacking — it just has disadvantage.
- The creature CAN move closer to the source if it has no other option (e.g., grappled and dragged).
- If the source is out of line of sight, the disadvantage and movement restriction don't apply (but the condition remains).

title: Grappled
summary: A grappled creature's speed becomes 0, and it can't benefit from any bonus to its speed.
content:
## Grappled

- A grappled creature's speed becomes **0**, and it can't benefit from any bonus to its speed.
- The condition ends if the grappler is **incapacitated**.
- The condition also ends if an effect removes the grappled creature from the reach of the grappler or grappling effect.

### Escaping a Grapple
- Use your **action** to make a **Strength (Athletics)** or **Dexterity (Acrobatics)** check contested by the grappler's **Strength (Athletics)** check.

### Important Notes
- Grappled does NOT impose disadvantage on attacks or ability checks.
- You CAN still attack, cast spells, and take other actions — you just can't move.
- The grappler can drag the grappled creature, but their own speed is halved while doing so.

title: Incapacitated
summary: An incapacitated creature can't take actions or reactions.
content:
## Incapacitated

- An incapacitated creature **can't take actions or reactions**.

### Important Notes
- This is a fundamental condition that many other conditions include (Paralyzed, Petrified, Stunned, Unconscious all include Incapacitated).
- The creature CAN still move (unlike Paralyzed or Unconscious).
- The creature CAN still speak (unlike Paralyzed or Unconscious).
- Concentration on a spell is NOT automatically broken by being incapacitated — only by specific conditions that say so, or by failing a concentration check from damage.

title: Invisible
summary: An invisible creature is impossible to see without the aid of magic or a special sense.
content:
## Invisible

- An invisible creature is impossible to see without the aid of magic or a special sense. For the purpose of hiding, the creature is **heavily obscured**. The creature's location can be detected by any noise it makes or any tracks it leaves.
- Attack rolls against the creature have **disadvantage**, and the creature's attack rolls have **advantage**.

### Common Sources
- *Invisibility* spell
- *Greater Invisibility* spell
- Potion of Invisibility
- Some class features (e.g., Ranger's Vanish)

### Important Notes
- Being invisible doesn't mean you can't be targeted — enemies can guess your location.
- Attacking or casting a spell that targets an enemy typically ends the *Invisibility* spell (but NOT *Greater Invisibility*).
- *See Invisibility*, *True Seeing*, and *Faerie Fire* can all counter invisibility.

title: Paralyzed
summary: A paralyzed creature is incapacitated and can't move or speak. Attacks against it have advantage and auto-crit from within 5 feet.
content:
## Paralyzed

- A paralyzed creature is **incapacitated** (can't take actions or reactions) and can't move or speak.
- The creature automatically **fails** Strength and Dexterity saving throws.
- Attack rolls against the creature have **advantage**.
- Any attack that hits the creature is a **critical hit** if the attacker is within 5 feet.

### Common Sources
- *Hold Person* spell (humanoids only)
- *Hold Monster* spell (any creature)
- Ghoul's Claws (Paralyzing Touch)
- Some poisons

### Important Notes
- This is one of the most devastating conditions in the game.
- The auto-crit from melee attacks makes paralysis extremely dangerous.
- Most paralysis effects allow a saving throw at the end of each turn to end the effect.

title: Petrified
summary: A petrified creature is transformed into a solid inanimate substance. It is incapacitated and has resistance to all damage.
content:
## Petrified

- A petrified creature is transformed, along with any nonmagical object it is wearing or carrying, into a solid inanimate substance (usually stone). Its weight increases by a factor of ten, and it ceases aging.
- The creature is **incapacitated**, can't move or speak, and is unaware of its surroundings.
- Attack rolls against the creature have **advantage**.
- The creature automatically **fails** Strength and Dexterity saving throws.
- The creature has **resistance to all damage**.
- The creature is **immune** to poison and disease (existing poison/disease is suspended, not neutralized).

### Common Sources
- Basilisk's Petrifying Gaze
- Medusa's Petrifying Gaze
- Cockatrice's Bite
- *Flesh to Stone* spell

### Recovery
- *Greater Restoration* spell
- *Wish* spell
- Specific story-driven cures

title: Poisoned
summary: A poisoned creature has disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks.
content:
## Poisoned

- A poisoned creature has **disadvantage** on attack rolls and ability checks.

### Common Sources
- Poison damage from monsters (e.g., Giant Spider, Pseudodragon)
- Poisoned weapons or darts
- *Ray of Sickness* spell
- Ingested poisons
- Failed Constitution saving throws against poison effects

### Important Notes
- Poisoned does NOT impose disadvantage on saving throws (only attacks and ability checks).
- Dwarves have advantage on saving throws against poison (Dwarven Resilience).
- The Monk's Purity of Body feature (10th level) makes you immune to poison and disease.
- *Lesser Restoration* and *Protection from Poison* can remove the poisoned condition.

title: Prone
summary: A prone creature's only movement option is to crawl. Melee attacks against it have advantage, ranged attacks have disadvantage.
content:
## Prone

- A prone creature's only movement option is to **crawl** (every foot of movement costs 1 extra foot), unless it stands up and thereby ends the condition.
- The creature has **disadvantage** on attack rolls.
- An attack roll against the creature has **advantage** if the attacker is within 5 feet. Otherwise, the attack roll has **disadvantage**.

### Standing Up
- Standing up costs an amount of movement equal to **half your speed**. For example, if your speed is 30 feet, you must spend 15 feet of movement to stand up.
- You can't stand up if you don't have enough movement left or if your speed is 0.

### Common Sources
- Being knocked prone by an attack or ability
- Tripping (Battlemaster's Trip Attack)
- Falling
- *Grease* spell
- Choosing to drop prone voluntarily (useful for avoiding ranged attacks)

### Tactical Notes
- Dropping prone can be a **defensive choice** against ranged attacks (they have disadvantage).
- But it makes you vulnerable to melee attacks (they have advantage).
- If you're already prone, crawling into cover can be very effective.

title: Restrained
summary: A restrained creature's speed becomes 0. It has disadvantage on attacks and DEX saves, and attacks against it have advantage.
content:
## Restrained

- A restrained creature's speed becomes **0**, and it can't benefit from any bonus to its speed.
- Attack rolls against the creature have **advantage**.
- The creature's attack rolls have **disadvantage**.
- The creature has **disadvantage** on Dexterity saving throws.

### Common Sources
- *Entangle* spell
- *Web* spell
- Net (weapon)
- Giant Spider's Web
- Some grappling effects

### Important Notes
- Restrained is similar to Grappled but worse — it also imposes disadvantage on attacks and DEX saves, and gives advantage to attackers.
- Unlike Grappled, Restrained often requires a specific action to escape (like a Strength check to break free from webs).

title: Stunned
summary: A stunned creature is incapacitated, can't move, and can speak only falteringly. Attacks against it have advantage.
content:
## Stunned

- A stunned creature is **incapacitated**, can't move, and can speak only falteringly.
- The creature automatically **fails** Strength and Dexterity saving throws.
- Attack rolls against the creature have **advantage**.

### Common Sources
- Monk's Stunning Strike (Ki feature)
- *Power Word Stun* spell
- *Divine Word* spell
- Mind Flayer's Mind Blast
- Some monster abilities

### Important Notes
- Stunned is very similar to Paralyzed, but does NOT grant auto-crits on melee attacks.
- Stunning Strike is one of the Monk's most powerful abilities because stun shuts down a target completely for at least one round.

title: Unconscious
summary: An unconscious creature is incapacitated, drops what it's holding, and falls prone. Melee hits are auto-crits.
content:
## Unconscious

- An unconscious creature is **incapacitated**, can't move or speak, and is unaware of its surroundings.
- The creature drops whatever it's holding and **falls prone**.
- The creature automatically **fails** Strength and Dexterity saving throws.
- Attack rolls against the creature have **advantage**.
- Any attack that hits the creature is a **critical hit** if the attacker is within 5 feet of the creature.

### Common Sources
- Dropping to 0 hit points
- *Sleep* spell
- Being knocked out (attacker's choice with melee)
- Some poisons or effects

### Important Notes
- When unconscious from 0 HP, you make **death saving throws** at the start of each turn.
- A melee attack against an unconscious creature at 0 HP causes an automatic death save failure (two failures if it crits — which it always does from within 5 feet).
- *Healing Word* is popular because it can wake up an unconscious ally from range as a bonus action.
